questions.stem: This medium-magnification liver section (special connective-tissue stain) shows blue-green bands of collagen surrounding rounded nodules of hepatocytes with disrupted lobular architecture. This histologic pattern is diagnostic of which condition?

explanation.summary: Cirrhosis is defined histologically by bridging fibrous septa (collagen produced by activated hepatic stellate cells) enclosing regenerative nodules of hepatocytes, disrupting normal lobular architecture and sinusoidal blood flow.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cirrhosis is defined histologically by bridging fibrous septa (collagen produced by activated hepatic stellate cells) enclosing regenerative nodules of hepatocytes, disrupting normal lobular architecture and sinusoidal blood flow.

questions.stem: This abdominal ultrasound of the liver demonstrates a small organ with coarse, heterogeneous echotexture and an irregular, nodular surface contour. These findings are most consistent with which diagnosis?

explanation.summary: In cirrhosis the liver becomes small with coarse echotexture and a nodular surface on ultrasound. Imaging supports the diagnosis but is not by itself sufficiently sensitive or specific to establish cirrhosis definitively.
explanation.whyCorrect: In cirrhosis the liver becomes small with coarse echotexture and a nodular surface on ultrasound. Imaging supports the diagnosis but is not by itself sufficiently sensitive or specific to establish cirrhosis definitively.

questions.stem: This cirrhotic patient has dilated, tortuous veins radiating outward from the umbilicus across the abdominal wall. This finding arises from portosystemic shunting through which recanalized structure?

explanation.summary: Caput medusae results from anastomosis between engorged paraumbilical veins and small epigastric veins of the abdominal wall, driven by portal hypertension. Recanalization of the paraumbilical/umbilical vein produces the visible radiating collaterals.
explanation.whyCorrect: Caput medusae results from anastomosis between engorged paraumbilical veins and small epigastric veins of the abdominal wall, driven by portal hypertension. Recanalization of the paraumbilical/umbilical vein produces the visible radiating collaterals.

questions.stem: This patient with cirrhosis shows marked, symmetric abdominal distention with bulging flanks from fluid within the peritoneal cavity. Which mechanism is the primary driver of this fluid accumulation?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bacterial gas production within the bowel

explanation.summary: Ascites in cirrhosis is driven chiefly by portal hypertension (raised hydrostatic pressure) together with hypoalbuminemia (low oncotic pressure) and renal sodium/water retention from systemic vasodilation. This produces tense abdominal distention with shifting dullness.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ascites in cirrhosis is driven chiefly by portal hypertension (raised hydrostatic pressure) together with hypoalbuminemia (low oncotic pressure) and renal sodium/water retention from systemic vasodilation. This produces tense abdominal distention with shifting dullness.
